passage:
A student is writing a policy brief recommending that school districts extend recess time for elementary students. The student has gathered the following notes:

• A 2019 study found that students who had at least 75 minutes of recess per week scored significantly higher on attention and behavioral regulation assessments.
• Finland, which consistently ranks among the top countries in international education assessments, gives students 15 minutes of outdoor break for every 45 minutes of instruction.
• Physical activity during childhood is associated with improved executive function, including working memory and cognitive flexibility.
• Recess time in U.S. schools has declined over the past 30 years as schools have increased instructional time in tested subjects.
• A survey of elementary teachers found that 79% believed students were more focused and better behaved after recess.
question: The student wants to argue that reducing recess has been counterproductive for academic goals. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.A: Finland provides students 15 minutes of outdoor break per 45 minutes of instruction and consistently ranks among the top countries in international education assessments.
choices.B: Because physical activity improves executive function and recess is associated with better attention and behavior, the decades-long trend of cutting recess in favor of more instructional time may actually undermine the academic focus it was intended to improve.
choices.C: Seventy-nine percent of elementary teachers report that students are more focused after recess, suggesting that teachers support increased recess time.
choices.D: U.S. schools have reduced recess over 30 years to increase instructional time in tested subjects, while Finland—which outperforms the U.S.—provides more break time.

explanation: The student's goal is to argue that reducing recess has been counterproductive for academic goals. Choice B does this most directly: it connects the research findings (physical activity improves executive function; recess improves attention and behavior) to the policy trend (cutting recess for more instructional time) and explicitly labels the consequence: the cut may 'undermine the academic focus it was intended to improve.' This directly states the counterproductive argument. Choice A uses the Finland comparison, which is suggestive but doesn't directly argue that U.S. recess cuts were counterproductive—it's an indirect implication. Choice C cites the teacher survey but frames it as teacher support rather than counterproductivity. Choice D uses both the U.S. trend and the Finland comparison but stops short of the explicit 'counterproductive' argument—it implies but does not state that cutting recess undermined academic goals.
